title: Acoustic bass guitar

infobox.value: (sometimes shortened to **acoustic bass** or initialized **ABG**) is a bass instrument with a hollow wooden body similar to, though usually larger than, a [steel-string acoustic guitar](/wiki/Steel-string_acoustic_guitar "Steel-string acoustic guitar"). Like the traditional [electric bass guitar](/wiki/Electric_bass_guitar "Electric bass guitar") and the [double bass](/wiki/Double_bass "Double bass"), the acoustic bass guitar commonly has four strings usually tuned E-A-D-G, an [octave](/wiki/Octave "Octave") below the lowest four strings of the 6-string [guitar](/wiki/Guitar "Guitar").

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Type of acoustic instrument

Not to be confused with [Double bass](/wiki/Double_bass "Double bass").

For other bass instruments, see [bass](/wiki/Bass_(instrument) "Bass (instrument)").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4a/Vea.guitarra.baixa.png/500px-Vea.guitarra.baixa.png)](/wiki/File:Vea.guitarra.baixa.png)

Two acoustic bass guitars

The **acoustic bass guitar** (sometimes shortened to **acoustic bass** or initialized **ABG**) is a bass instrument with a hollow wooden body similar to, though usually larger than, a [steel-string acoustic guitar](/wiki/Steel-string_acoustic_guitar "Steel-string acoustic guitar"). Like the traditional [electric bass guitar](/wiki/Electric_bass_guitar "Electric bass guitar") and the [double bass](/wiki/Double_bass "Double bass"), the acoustic bass guitar commonly has four strings usually tuned E-A-D-G, an [octave](/wiki/Octave "Octave") below the lowest four strings of the 6-string [guitar](/wiki/Guitar "Guitar").

Because it can sometimes be difficult to hear an acoustic bass guitar without an [amplifier](/wiki/Instrument_amplifier "Instrument amplifier") even in settings with other acoustic instruments, most acoustic basses have [pickups](/wiki/Pick_up_(music_technology) "Pick up (music technology)"), either magnetic or [piezoelectric](/wiki/Piezoelectric_pickup "Piezoelectric pickup") or both, so they can be amplified with a [bass amp](/wiki/Bass_amp "Bass amp").

Traditional [music of Mexico](/wiki/Music_of_Mexico "Music of Mexico") features several varieties of acoustic bass guitars such as the **[guitarrón](/wiki/Guitarr%C3%B3n_mexicano "Guitarrón mexicano")**, a very large, deep-bodied Mexican 6-string acoustic bass guitar played in [Mariachi](/wiki/Mariachi "Mariachi") bands, the **[león](/wiki/Leona_(instrument) "Leona (instrument)")**, plucked with a pick, and the **[bajo sexto](/wiki/Bajo_sexto "Bajo sexto")**, with six pairs of strings. This type of guitar is also known as **Bajoloche**, played with a style that includes slapping or plucking the strings. Arsenal Effectivo and Herencia de Patrones helped popularize bajoloche.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Acoustic_bass_guitar&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/65/Estonbass.jpg/120px-Estonbass.jpg)](/wiki/File:Estonbass.jpg)

[Eston](/wiki/Eko_guitars "Eko guitars") acoustic bass guitar with no electric pickup, fretless but with fretlike markers, made in Italy in the 1980s

The Bassoguitar built by the [Regal Musical Instrument Company](/wiki/Regal_Musical_Instrument_Company "Regal Musical Instrument Company") was likely the first mass-produced acoustic bass to make use of a guitar-like body.[[1]](#cite_note-1) Harptone started producing their B4 model in 1965 under the name Supreme; production ended in 1975. They also made a very limited number under the [Standel](/wiki/Standel "Standel") logo. [Ernie Ball](/wiki/Ernie_Ball "Ernie Ball") began producing a model in the early 70's. Ball's aim was to provide bass guitarists with a more acoustic-sounding instrument that would match better with the sound of [acoustic guitars](/wiki/Acoustic_guitar "Acoustic guitar"). Ball stated that "...if there were electric bass guitars to go with [electric guitars](/wiki/Electric_guitar "Electric guitar") then you ought to have acoustic basses to go with acoustic guitars." Ball said that "...the closest thing to an acoustic bass was the [Mexican guitarron](/wiki/Guitarr%C3%B3n_mexicano "Guitarrón mexicano")...in mariachi bands, so I bought one down in [Tijuana](/wiki/Tijuana "Tijuana") and tinkered with it."[[2]](#cite_note-ernieball.com-2)

Ball collaborated with [George William Fullerton](/wiki/George_William_Fullerton "George William Fullerton"), a former employee at [Fender](/wiki/Fender_Musical_Instruments_Corporation "Fender Musical Instruments Corporation"), to develop the [Earthwood](/wiki/Earthwood "Earthwood") acoustic bass guitar, which was introduced in 1972. Production of this instrument ceased in 1974, resuming a few years later under the direction of Ernie Ball's employee Dan Norton, until production finally ended again in 1985. The Earthwood acoustic bass guitar was quite large and deep in contrast to most instruments in current production, which gave it more volume, especially in the low register.[photo 1](https://web.archive.org/web/20070313231342/http://www.reyesaccordions.com/Images/Earthwood77.jpg) The Ernie Ball company describes Ball's design as "an idea before its time"; the instrument was little used in acoustic musical performances until the late 1980s, when the acoustic basses were used in performances on the *[MTV Unplugged](/wiki/MTV_Unplugged "MTV Unplugged")* television program.[[2]](#cite_note-ernieball.com-2) The Earthwood was quickly supplanted by the Washburn AB-40 designed by Mick Donner and Richard Siegle. The AB-40 and the more affordable AB-20 became the instrument of choice for bass players appearing on *Unplugged*.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

Folk bass player [Ashley Hutchings](/wiki/Ashley_Hutchings "Ashley Hutchings") used the acoustic bass guitar with his [Etchingham Steam Band](/wiki/Etchingham_Steam_Band "Etchingham Steam Band") in 1974 and 1975.[[4]](#cite_note-4) An early user of the acoustic bass guitar in rock was English multi-instrumentalist and composer [Mike Oldfield](/wiki/Mike_Oldfield "Mike Oldfield"), who had one custom-built for him by luthier [Tony Zemaitis](/wiki/Tony_Zemaitis "Tony Zemaitis") in the mid-1970s and used the bass on a number of his recordings from that time onwards, a prominent example being his 1975 album *[Ommadawn](/wiki/Ommadawn "Ommadawn")*.

## Construction and tuning

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Acoustic_bass_guitar&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Construction and tuning")]

Like the traditional electric bass and the double bass, the acoustic bass guitar commonly has four strings, which are normally tuned E-A-D-G, an octave below the lowest four strings of the 6-string [guitar](/wiki/Guitar "Guitar"). Like the electric bass guitar, models with five or more strings have been produced, although these are less common. In part, this is because the body of an acoustic bass guitar is too small to produce a [resonance](/wiki/Resonance "Resonance") of acceptable volume at lower pitches on the low "B" string. One solution uses the five string acoustic bass to add an additional high string ("E-A-D-G-C") instead of adding a low "B". Another solution is to rely on amplification to reproduce the low "B" string's notes, or by making the body a little bit larger.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ce/Washburn_AB-10.jpg/120px-Washburn_AB-10.jpg)](/wiki/File:Washburn_AB-10.jpg)

[Washburn](/wiki/Washburn_Guitars "Washburn Guitars") AB-10 [acoustic-electric](/wiki/Acoustic-electric_guitar "Acoustic-electric guitar") bass guitar

There are also [semi-acoustic](/wiki/Semi-acoustic "Semi-acoustic") models, fitted with pickups, for use with an amplifier. The soundbox of these instruments is not large enough to amplify the sound. Instead, it produces a distinctive tone when amplified, similarly to semi-acoustic electric guitars. Thin-body semi-acoustic basses such as the violin-shaped [Höfner](/wiki/H%C3%B6fner "Höfner") made famous by the early [Beatles](/wiki/The_Beatles "The Beatles") and several [Fender](/wiki/Fender_Musical_Instruments_Corporation "Fender Musical Instruments Corporation") models are not normally regarded as acoustic basses at all, but rather as hollow-bodied bass guitars. There are also semi-acoustic basses such as [Godin Guitars](/wiki/Godin_(Guitar_Manufacturer) "Godin (Guitar Manufacturer)")' "A-Series" that, once amplified, sound much closer between acoustic bass guitars and [upright basses](/wiki/Double_bass "Double bass") (double bass), and have been used in professional circles to "simulate" one when it would be impractical for transportation and other reasons to use a full-sized upright bass. As with semi-acoustic electric guitars, the line between acoustic instruments fitted with pickups and electric instruments with tone-enhancing bodies is sometimes hard to draw. Some instruments can also be equipped with a variety of pickups such as [piezo](/wiki/Piezoelectric_sensor "Piezoelectric sensor") pickups, the "standard" of acoustic-electric instruments as well as [synth](/wiki/Synthesizer "Synthesizer") pickups that can replay "virtual" upright bass sounds and bring a semi-acoustic bass much closer to a double bass sonically.

[Saga Musical Instruments](/wiki/Saga_Musical_Instruments "Saga Musical Instruments") produces a four-string bass [resonator guitar](/wiki/Resonator_guitar "Resonator guitar") under their *[Regal](/wiki/Regal_Musical_Instrument_Company "Regal Musical Instrument Company")* brand name.[videos](https://web.archive.org/web/20070715071841/http://www.folkofthewood.com/page2517.htm) [National Reso-Phonic Guitars](/wiki/National_Reso-Phonic_Guitars "National Reso-Phonic Guitars") also produce three models of resonator bass guitar.[[5]](#cite_note-5) One notable player of the resonator bass guitar is [Les Claypool](/wiki/Les_Claypool "Les Claypool") of [Primus](/wiki/Primus_(band) "Primus (band)"), who plays Regal RD-05 resonator basses.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

## Manufacturers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Acoustic_bass_guitar&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Manufacturers")]

Other manufacturers of acoustic bass guitars (not mentioned above) include [Alvarez](/wiki/Alvarez_Guitars "Alvarez Guitars"), [Baton Rouge](/w/index.php?title=Baton_Rouge_Guitars&action=edit&redlink=1 "Baton Rouge Guitars (page does not exist)"), [Boulder Creek Guitars](/w/index.php?title=Boulder_Creek_Guitars&action=edit&redlink=1 "Boulder Creek Guitars (page does not exist)"), [Breedlove](/wiki/Breedlove_Guitars "Breedlove Guitars"), [Chesbro Music Company (Teton Guitars)](/wiki/Chesbro_Music_Company "Chesbro Music Company"), [Cort](/wiki/Cort_Guitars "Cort Guitars"), [Crafter](/w/index.php?title=Crafter_(guitar_manufacturer)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Crafter (guitar manufacturer) (page does not exist)"), [Jerzey](/w/index.php?title=Jerzey&action=edit&redlink=1 "Jerzey (page does not exist)"), [Dean](/wiki/Dean_Guitars "Dean Guitars"), [Eko](/wiki/Eko_guitars "Eko guitars"), [Epiphone](/wiki/Epiphone "Epiphone"), [Eston](/wiki/Eston_guitars "Eston guitars"), [Fender](/wiki/Fender_Musical_Instruments_Corporation "Fender Musical Instruments Corporation"), [Furch](/wiki/Furch_Guitars "Furch Guitars"), [Gibson](/wiki/Gibson_Guitars "Gibson Guitars"), [Gold Tone](/w/index.php?title=Gold_Tone_Music_Groupe&action=edit&redlink=1 "Gold Tone Music Groupe (page does not exist)"), [Guild](/wiki/Guild_Guitar_Company "Guild Guitar Company"), [Ibanez](/wiki/Ibanez "Ibanez"), [Larrivée](/wiki/Larriv%C3%A9e_(guitar_company) "Larrivée (guitar company)"), [Lindo Guitars](/w/index.php?title=Lindo_Guitars&action=edit&redlink=1 "Lindo Guitars (page does not exist)"), [Luna Guitars](/wiki/Luna_Guitars "Luna Guitars"), [Martin](/wiki/C.F._Martin_%26_Company "C.F. Martin & Company"), [Maton](/wiki/Maton "Maton"), [Michael Kelly](/wiki/Michael_Kelly_Guitars "Michael Kelly Guitars"), [Oscar Schmidt by Washburn](/wiki/Oscar_Schmidt_Inc. "Oscar Schmidt Inc."), [Ovation](/wiki/Ovation_Guitar "Ovation Guitar") and its subsidiary Applause, [Ozark](/w/index.php?title=Ozark_Music&action=edit&redlink=1 "Ozark Music (page does not exist)"), [Prestige](/w/index.php?title=Prestige_guitars&action=edit&redlink=1 "Prestige guitars (page does not exist)"), [Ribbecke Halfling Bass](/w/index.php?title=Ribbecke_Halfling_Bass&action=edit&redlink=1 "Ribbecke Halfling Bass (page does not exist)"), [Samick](/wiki/Samick "Samick"), [Sigma Guitars](/wiki/Sigma_Guitars "Sigma Guitars"), [Sunlite](/w/index.php?title=Sunlite&action=edit&redlink=1 "Sunlite (page does not exist)"), Sandberg, [Spector](/wiki/Spector_(company) "Spector (company)"), [Stagg](/wiki/Stagg_Music "Stagg Music"), [Tagima](/wiki/Tagima "Tagima"), [Tacoma](/wiki/Tacoma_Guitars "Tacoma Guitars"), [Takamine](/wiki/Takamine_Guitars "Takamine Guitars"), [Tanglewood](/wiki/Tanglewood_Guitars "Tanglewood Guitars"), [Taylor](/wiki/Taylor_Guitars "Taylor Guitars"), [Vintage Guitars](/w/index.php?title=Vintage_Guitars_(brand)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Vintage Guitars (brand) (page does not exist)"), [Washburn](/wiki/Washburn_guitars "Washburn guitars") and [Warwick](/wiki/Warwick_(bass_guitar) "Warwick (bass guitar)").

## Mexican bass guitars

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Acoustic_bass_guitar&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Mexican bass guitars")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/70/AcousticBassGtr.jpg/250px-AcousticBassGtr.jpg)](/wiki/File:AcousticBassGtr.jpg)

Mexican [guitarrón](/wiki/Guitarr%C3%B3n_mexicano "Guitarrón mexicano") acoustic bass guitar

Traditional music of Mexico features varieties of acoustic bass guitars. The [guitarrón](/wiki/Guitarr%C3%B3n_mexicano "Guitarrón mexicano") is a very large, deep-bodied Mexican 6-string acoustic bass guitar played in Mariachi bands. The [bajo sexto](/wiki/Bajo_sexto "Bajo sexto"), with six pairs of strings, resembles a twelve-string guitar tuned an octave lower. The heavy gauge strings generate a large string tension, yet the guitar is built relatively lightly. Musicians began to adopt the bajo sexto in [Texas](/wiki/Texas "Texas") in the 1920s with the rise of "[Tex-Mex](/wiki/Tejano_music "Tejano music")" music, and it remains in common use for parts that would be played by the [piano](/wiki/Piano "Piano") in traditional [American popular music](/wiki/American_popular_music "American popular music"). The tuning of these instruments is (capital letters are an octave lower than small letters):

:   Baja sexto: Ee Aa Dd Gg Cc Ff

Other Latin American acoustic bass guitars exist as well, such as the [Bordonua](/wiki/Bordonua "Bordonua").
